title: Why Holidays Are Triggering for Trauma Survivors
researcherTopic: yoga and consciousness, the mechanics of the mind
metaDescription: For trauma survivors, the holidays can be a minefield of triggers. Learn why forced cheer feels so brutal and discover how to navigate the season with somatic sovereignty.
imageDescription: Warm, golden light streams through a window, illuminating the delicate, repaired cracks of a kintsugi bowl. The scene is quiet and contemplative, evoking a sense of gentle healing and the beauty of imperfection. The focus is on the golden repair, a symbol of resilience and the art of mending.
htmlContent:
The most wonderful time of the year is often the most brutal. For those of us who carry the weight of a past that will not stay in the past, the forced cheer and relentless performance of family harmony can feel like a special kind of violence, a collective gaslighting that leaves us feeling more alone than ever. We are asked to come to the table, to smile and pass the gravy, all while a war rages within the silent confines of our own nervous systems.

The Architecture of the Holiday Trigger

To understand why the holidays dismantle our carefully constructed sense of stability, we must first appreciate the architecture of a trigger. It is not the memory itself that does the damage, but the sudden, involuntary regression to the emotional and physiological state of the original trauma. The brain, as modern neuroscience confirms, does not store the past in a linear, orderly fashion. Instead, it is a web of associations, a network where a sensory input... the specific shade of red in a Santa Claus costume, the sound of a particular Christmas carol, the cloying sweetness of a holiday dessert - can activate an entire constellation of terror, helplessness, or shame that has been dormant for months. This is not a failure of will or a lack of gratitude; it is the predictable functioning of a nervous system that has been rewired by overwhelming experience. It is the body remembering what the mind has tried so desperately to forget.

The Tyranny of Forced Joy

The holidays are a masterclass in cognitive dissonance. We are bombarded with messages of joy, connection, and belonging, while our internal reality is one of hypervigilance and dread. This chasm between the cultural mandate and our lived experience is where so much of the suffering lies. It is not just the presence of triggers, but the added layer of shame for not feeling the way we are “supposed” to feel. We watch others seemingly revel in the festivities, and the old, familiar story of our own otherness, our own fundamental difference, gets reinforced. That becomes the tyranny of forced joy, a cultural demand that we perform a state of being that is not authentically available to us. It is a form of emotional labor that is both exhausting and strikingly invalidating.

That becomes not an invitation to wallow in misery, but a call to a more courageous form of presence. It is the willingness to meet the discomfort, to allow it to be there without needing it to change. It is in this space of allowing that the charge begins to dissipate, that the feeling reveals its transient nature. It is a practice, not a perfect performance. For a deeper look at this, consider reading our article on The Myth of the A place where falling apart is permitted.

Navigating the Minefield: Strategies for Somatic Sovereignty

If we cannot control the external environment of the holidays, we can at least cultivate a sense of sovereignty within our own skin. What emerges is not about building higher walls or armoring ourselves against the world, but about developing a more intimate and compassionate relationship with our own nervous system. It begins with the radical act of giving ourselves permission to have boundaries. This might look like leaving a gathering early, skipping an event altogether, or simply taking a few moments to be alone in a quiet room. It is about honoring the body’s signals of overwhelm before they escalate into a full-blown shutdown or explosion. A client once described this to me as learning to be her own loving parent, the one she never had, the one who says, “I see you’re struggling. Let’s go home.”

Let's talk about sensory triggers specifically. The smell of a certain cologne. The sound of a particular song. The texture of a fabric. The quality of light at a certain time of day. These aren't "just" memories. They're stored in the most primitive parts of your brain - the amygdala, the olfactory bulb, the thalamus - and they bypass all rational processing.

Bessel van der Kolk's brain imaging studies showed this clearly. When trauma survivors are exposed to triggers, the Broca's area - the part of the brain responsible for speech - goes offline. Literally. The language center shuts down. That's why you can't explain what's happening when you're triggered. That's why "use your words" is such an impossible demand in that state. There are no words available. Only sensation.

If this resonates with you, I want you to know something. You're not crazy. You're not weak. You're not making it up. Your brain is doing exactly what it was designed to do - protect you from threats it has encountered before. The work isn't to stop the triggering. The work is to help your nervous system update its files. To learn that the smell, the sound, the texture - they belong to then. And then is over. Even if your body hasn't gotten the memo yet.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link).

We can read all the books and articles in the world, but until we begin to translate that knowledge into a felt sense in the body, it remains just a collection of interesting ideas. The holidays, in all their triggering glory, are a powerful laboratory for this work of integration. They offer us a direct, unmediated experience of our own wiring, and an opportunity to meet it with something other than our habitual reactivity. That becomes the essence of what we explore in our article on How to the Rupture and Repair.

Seriously.

The Ghosts of Holidays Past

For many of us, the holidays are haunted. They are populated by the ghosts of family members who are no longer with us, by the echoes of arguments that happened decades ago, and by the palpable absence of the love and safety we craved but never received. These are not literal ghosts, of course, but the energetic imprints of unresolved grief and trauma. They are the reason why the same family dynamics tend to play out year after year, why we find ourselves slipping into old, familiar roles without even realizing it. We become the peacemaker, the invisible child, the scapegoat, the overachiever. These roles were once survival strategies, ways of navigating a family system that was chaotic, unpredictable, or dangerous. The problem is that they are no longer needed, yet they continue to run our lives on autopilot.

We can understand this intellectually, but the real freedom comes from the direct experience of it in our own bodies. It is the felt sense of release that comes from finally letting go of the hope that the past could have been any different. It is the spaciousness that opens up when we are no longer at war with our own history. What emerges is the path of true healing and consciousness, a road we explore in more detail in our article on The Body Keeps the Score is Not a Metaphor.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link).

Rewriting the Holiday Script

The ultimate act of healing is not to simply survive the holidays, but to begin to rewrite the script. What emerges is not about pretending that the past didn't happen, but about choosing to no longer be defined by it. It is about recognizing that we have a choice in how we respond to the inevitable triggers and challenges. We can choose to react from the wounded place of the inner child, or we can choose to respond from the grounded, compassionate place of the inner adult. This is the essence of self-leadership, the capacity to work through the messiness of our inner and outer worlds with wisdom and grace. It is the understanding that our true power lies not in controlling our circumstances, but in choosing our response to them.

Are you willing to be uncomfortable for longer than feels reasonable? Because that is what this asks. Not permanent discomfort. But the willingness to stay in the difficult space long enough for something to shift. Most people leave too early. The question is whether you will.

I want to pause here and check in with you. How's your body doing right now? Not your mind - your body. Is there tension somewhere? A heaviness? A restlessness? Whatever you find, it's information. It's your nervous system responding to what you're reading. And that response is valid.

One thing I've learned in my own recovery is that reading about trauma can be activating. Your body doesn't always know the difference between remembering something and experiencing it. So if you need to take a break, take a break. Put your feet on the floor. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. This isn't avoidance. This is regulation. There's a difference.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do I feel so exhausted after holiday gatherings, even if nothing bad happened?

What we find here is often the result of hypervigilance, the state of being constantly on alert for potential threats. Even if no overt conflict occurs, your nervous system is working overtime to scan the environment for danger, which is incredibly draining. It's the energetic cost of being in a space that your body does not register as truly safe.

Is it selfish to set boundaries during the holidays?

Setting boundaries is not selfish; it is an act of self-preservation and a prerequisite for genuine connection. When we override our own needs to please others, we build resentment, not relationship. A clear, kind boundary is a gift to both yourself and the other person, creating the possibility for more authentic interaction.

A guided journal specifically designed for trauma recovery is Trauma Recovery Journal (paid link).

How can I deal with family members who don't understand or respect my trauma?

You cannot make someone understand something they are not ready or willing to see. The work is not to convince them, but to hold your own reality and protect your own peace. This may mean limiting contact, avoiding certain topics of conversation, or having an exit strategy in place for when you feel overwhelmed.

Stay with me here.

I feel guilty for not feeling more grateful during the holidays. What's wrong with me?

Nothing is wrong with you. Gratitude is a beautiful state, but it cannot be forced. When your nervous system is in a state of threat, the capacity for gratitude is simply not available. The most compassionate thing you can do is to acknowledge the truth of your experience, without adding a layer of self-judgment.

The process through the holiday season, for those of us who carry the weight of the past, is not a simple one. It is a path that asks for immense courage, for a willingness to feel what is difficult, and for a radical commitment to our own well-being. It is the understanding that the greatest gift we can give ourselves is the gift of our own presence, our own compassion, our own unwavering commitment to the truth of our own experience. It is the slow, steady return to the quiet wisdom of the body, the one place where true safety can ever be found.

A discreet grounding tool you can stick anywhere is Calm Strips Sensory Stickers (paid link).

This return is not a destination, but a direction. It is the conscious and deliberate turning of attention, again and again, to the felt sense of the body. It is the quiet recognition that the sensations of tightness, of heat, of emptiness, are not enemies to be vanquished, but messengers to be heard. They are the echoes of a younger self, a self that did whatever it had to do to survive. To meet these echoes with tenderness, with curiosity, with a willingness to listen - this is the heart of the practice. It is the slow and steady work of reclaiming the abandoned territories of the self, of weaving a fabric of wholeness from the frayed threads of the past. It is the most genuine act of love.
